title: Grilled octopus
subtitle: Greek-style chargrilled octopus with olive oil and lemon
excerpt: Octopus simmered until tender, then finished over a very hot grill or griddle until the tentacles char and crisp at the edges. Dressed with good olive oil, lemon, dried oregano, and a scatter of flat parsley. One of the great dishes of Greek coastal cooking.
sourceNotes: Grilled octopus is documented in Greek coastal cooking from antiquity. The technique of boiling before grilling is well established in both Greek and Italian fish cookery as the correct way to tenderise octopus before applying high heat. In Greece, octopus is hung in the sun to dry before grilling, a technique that draws out moisture and concentrates the flavour.

content.text: Octopus has one enemy: wrong texture. It is either perfectly tender or unpleasantly chewy depending on how long it cooks. The two paths to tender octopus are low-and-slow (a 2-hour braise) or flash-fast (raw, straight over a hot grill, under 2 minutes per side). The Greek restaurant method (poach first, grill to finish) takes the best of both: 90 minutes of gentle simmering makes the flesh tender throughout, and 4 minutes over a ripping hot grill chars the tentacles and crisps the skin.

Buy frozen octopus if you can: the freeze-thaw cycle breaks down the muscle fibres and reduces cooking time. No salt in the poaching water. It toughens the flesh. A cork in the pot is traditional lore; it has no proven effect on tenderness but does no harm.

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: juice of 1, 1 in wedges to serve

content.text: Place the {{octopus}} in a large pot with the {{bay-leaves}} and enough cold water to just cover. Do not add salt. Bring to a simmer and poach gently for 60 to 90 minutes, a 1 kg octopus needs about 75 minutes. Test by piercing the thickest part of a tentacle with a knife: it should slide in with no resistance. Remove and allow to cool briefly. The octopus can now be refrigerated for up to 24 hours.

Cut the poached octopus into portions, tentacles separated, head halved. Brush with a little of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}. Heat a grill or griddle pan to its maximum temperature. Grill the octopus pieces for 2 minutes per side until the edges char and the skin crisps and blisters. Place on a serving plate. Whisk the remaining olive oil with the {{lemon}} juice and {{red-wine-vinegar}}, pour over the octopus, scatter with {{oregano-dried}}, {{parsley-flat}}, and a grind of {{black-pepper}}. Serve with lemon wedges.

items.fix: Always test with a knife before removing; poach at a gentle simmer, not a boil; frozen octopus reliably needs 60 minutes, fresh can take 90
items.cause: Undercooked or poached at a rolling boil
items.symptom: Octopus is rubbery and chewy after poaching

items.fix: If the octopus is very tender, brush with oil and grill for only 1 minute per side, just enough for char marks and crisping
items.cause: Poached too long, then aggressive heat
items.symptom: Octopus falls apart on the grill

items.fix: The pan must be at its maximum temperature and smoking before the octopus goes on; do not add oil to the pan, oil the octopus instead
items.symptom: No char or crisping on the skin

items.fix: Season the dressing assertively; the octopus needs enough olive oil, lemon, and salt in the finish to make up for the salt-free poach
items.cause: Poached in unsalted water, and dressing was too light

content.text: Octopus drying on a line in the sun (tentacles splayed against a whitewashed wall) is one of the defining images of Greek coastal villages. The drying was practical before refrigeration: it draws moisture from the flesh and concentrates the flavour. The grilled result, dressed in olive oil and lemon, is the standard mezze of any seaside taverna from May to October. It appears alongside taramasalata, olives, and grilled bread as the beginning of a meal, or as a main course with a salad. The technique of boiling before grilling is shared across the Italian Adriatic coast and the entire eastern Mediterranean wherever octopus is eaten.
